🌏 Shenzhen AI Study Trip

Pre-read for the NUHS-GMIO NCIS delegation · 19-22 July 2026 · Updated 15 Jul per Sing-China info session briefing
Who
Dennis · Jane · Marissa
When
Sun 19 → Wed 22 Jul
Where
Shenzhen, China
Sponsor
NCIS

📋 Trip at a Glance

"We want to understand how Shenzhen turns AI from demo into production - and what parts can realistically be adapted into Singapore's Epic + Synapxe-governed healthcare environment."

Five stops, five different lenses · SZPH and Guangming confirmed per 15 Jul briefing

🎮
Tencent
Mon 20 AM · HQ tour + healthcare solutions
🛡️
Ping An
Mon 20 PM · AI healthcare + elderly care
🏥
Shenzhen People's Hospital
Tue 21 AM · Hospital tour
🧬
Guangming Life Science Park
Tue 21 PM · Research teams + ecosystem
🦝
XtalPi
Wed 22 AM · AI + robotics + cancer screening

Top 3 things to remember

1
WeChat-style front doors and Ping An-style longitudinal care are operating-system level plays, not single AI tools.
2
The proof-of-concept stop is Shenzhen People's Hospital — a tour of real clinical scale, not a vendor demo.
3
The translational piece is Guangming — government infrastructure shapes how fast research reaches patients.
Binhai Mansion · Tencent HQ

2. Tencent

Binhai Mansion, Nanshan District · WeChat + AI agent ecosystem + Tencent Healthcare

⚡ Fun fact

The "Ten" in Tencent comes from "Teng" (腾) — the same character in founder Ma Huateng's name, which also means "to soar." His first pot of gold came from building a hardware dongle that displayed real-time stock prices.[8] Tencent owns the world's largest single-app social platform (WeChat / Weixin, 1.4B+ combined monthly active users)[5] and its AI Medical Innovation System (AIMIS / Miying) assists doctors in screening for diabetic retinopathy, lung cancer, and esophageal cancer.[6] In March 2026, Tencent integrated AI agent capabilities into WeChat, allowing direct AI interaction through the messaging interface.[7]
Read more

🔍 Why it matters

  • Tencent's healthcare power comes from its ecosystem: WeChat, mini-programs, payments, cloud, AI, digital identity, patient engagement, and medical AI tools.
  • Products include WeChat Intelligent Healthcare, Tencent Doctorwork, and AIMIS / Miying - the AI medical imaging & assisted diagnosis platform. AIMIS supports screening for diabetic retinopathy, lung cancer, and esophageal cancer.
  • In March 2026, Tencent launched an AI agent tool integrated with WeChat - direct AI interaction through WeChat's messaging interface.

🔍 What to observe

Digital front door
Can patients book, pay, ask, learn, manage follow-up, and navigate in one app?
AI patient engagement
Triage, FAQ, symptom checking, education, reminders, appointment nav?
AI agent workflow
Cross-service tasks, or just a chatbot?
Data permission
How do patients authorise data/image sharing?
Integration
How tightly connected to hospital systems?

💬 Questions to ask

1How do you decide which healthcare journey is suitable for AI agent automation?
2Is the AI agent connected to real hospital workflows, or mainly patient-facing?
3How do you prevent the AI from giving unsafe medical advice?
4How do clinicians review, override, or audit AI recommendations?
5For imaging AI, how is model performance monitored after deployment?
6How do you manage patient consent when images/reports are shared across institutions?
7What are the biggest barriers when scaling AI across hospitals?
8If a healthcare system already has a major EMR, how would Tencent integrate without disrupting it?

🇸🇬 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Tencent is highly relevant to NUHS App / MyChart / digital front door thinking. Singapore already has Epic/MyChart + strong governance, so we cannot simply copy WeChat. But we can learn how Tencent designs patient engagement at scale.

  • AI-powered cancer patient navigation
  • Treatment journey reminders and education
  • Cancer care plan digital engagement
  • AI-assisted appointment and follow-up guidance
  • Patient-facing FAQ or concierge integrated with approved content
Singapore already has Epic/MyChart and strong governance, so we cannot simply copy WeChat's model. But we can learn how Tencent designs patient engagement at scale - AI-supported navigation, education, reminders, appointment access, and patient-facing service orchestration.
SZPH
渝市人民医院

3. Shenzhen People's Hospital

Luohu District, Shenzhen · Guided hospital tour — operations, facilities and clinical scale

⚡ Fun fact

Founded in 1946 as Bao'an County Health Center, Shenzhen People's Hospital became Shenzhen's first Grade 3A hospital in 1994, and was named a Guangdong "Smart Hospital" in 2017. It is affiliated with Southern University of Science and Technology (first hospital) and Jinan University (second clinical college), with 2,500 beds and 5,000+ staff. The trip's scope here is a guided tour of operations and facilities — the focus is real clinical scale, not a vendor AI demo.[SZPH]
Read more

🔍 Why it matters

  • Founded 1946, first Grade 3A in Shenzhen (1994), named a Guangdong "Smart Hospital" in 2017 — one of the largest, oldest and most operationally mature public hospitals in the city.
  • The briefing scopes this stop as a guided hospital tour — operations and facilities, not a vendor AI pitch — so the value here is real clinical scale and patient flow.
  • As a teaching hospital with strong research infrastructure, it shows how a Chinese public Grade 3A hospital runs inpatient throughput, training, and research under one roof.

🔍 What to observe

Scale
2,500 beds, 5,000+ staff, 3.63M outpatient visits (2019) — what design choices hold up at this volume?
Patient flow
How OPD, inpatient, emergency, surgery are routed — and what the last big redesign was
Smart-hospital layers
Which "smart" capabilities are clinical-grade vs admin-only
Teaching integration
How residency, research, and clinical service co-exist
Leadership signals
What the Party Secretary / President chose to show us on the tour

💬 Questions to ask

1Of all the "smart hospital" capabilities you run, which 3 actually move the needle on patient outcomes today?
2How is patient flow designed across OPD / inpatient / emergency — and what was the last big redesign?
3For a hospital this large, how do you measure and reward clinical quality at department level?
4How do you keep residency training sustainable when service volume is high?
5What is the role of AI in your day-to-day operations today — and where have you explicitly chosen not to use it?
6How does the hospital coordinate with community health centres for chronic disease follow-up?
7What is your biggest workforce challenge in the next 3 years — and is AI part of the answer?
8For a visiting Singapore team, what would you most want us to learn from how you operate?

🇸🇬 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: This is the trip's only clinical on-the-ground stop. If NUHS wants to understand how a large Chinese public hospital actually runs, this is where that question gets answered.

  • Patient-flow design at 2,500-bed scale
  • Smart-hospital capabilities that actually changed clinician workload (not demos)
  • Teaching-research-clinical integration model
  • Workforce planning in a high-volume public hospital
  • Community-hospital handoff for chronic disease follow-up
  • Governance of "smart" features — what is approved vs shelved
For NCIS / NUHS, the honest value of this stop is operational realism — how a large Chinese public hospital makes patient flow, residency training, and "smart hospital" features cohere at 2,500-bed scale, and which parts of that discipline (not the AI) could lift a Singapore teaching hospital.
Ping An Finance Centre · Futian

4. Ping An

Ping An Finance Centre, Futian District · AI healthcare + elderly care + AI impact on workforce, staffing and ethics

⚡ Fun fact

Ping An is China's first joint-stock insurance company, founded in 1988 in Shekou[11] — a tiny fishing village that later became known as "China's Silicon Valley." It started with just 42 million CNY (~USD 6 million) registered capital[11] and is now a Fortune Global 500 company.[13] Its health arm, Ping An Good Doctor, has 400M registered users[13] — larger than the US population. It is not a hospital operator; it is a health-tech platform processing appointments, teleconsultations, and prescriptions at scale.
Read more

🔍 Why it matters

  • Ping An connects insurance, health services, online consultation, health management, pharmacies, senior care, and AI into one ecosystem.
  • Ping An Healthcare / Ping An Good Doctor offers online consultations, hospital referrals, appointments, health management, wellness services. The broader ecosystem includes hospital, doctor, pharmacy, and elderly care partnerships.
  • In 2025, Ping An Healthcare announced its AI model and doctor platform had built-in access to DeepSeek's model[12] to support diagnosis and personalised health management.

🔍 What to observe

Health ecosystem
Insurance + care + pharmacy + online consult connection?
Risk prediction
How they identify high-risk patients
Care coordination
AI for follow-up + longitudinal care
Doctor platform
How doctors use AI in real work
Patient engagement
Nudges toward care actions
Financing link
Payment/insurance data supports healthcare planning

💬 Questions to ask

1How do you combine healthcare, insurance, pharmacy, and patient engagement data safely?
2What AI use cases are most mature: risk prediction, triage, care management, claims, doctor assistance?
3How do you validate AI outputs used by doctors?
4How do you avoid over-reliance on AI recommendations?
5How do you measure healthcare outcome improvement, not just platform usage?
6What are the strongest use cases for oncology or chronic disease?
7How do you personalise care without creating privacy or bias concerns?
8How do you manage AI governance across such a large ecosystem?

🇸🇬 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: Ping An is probably one of the most relevant for NCIS oncology population health.

  • Cancer risk stratification
  • Survivorship follow-up
  • Longitudinal care management
  • Patient adherence and digital nudging
  • Predictive analytics for high-risk cancer patients
  • AI-supported care coordination across hospital and community
For NCIS, the most useful learning may not be insurance itself, but Ping An's ability to connect longitudinal patient data, predictive analytics, digital engagement, and care coordination into a scalable operating model.
GM
光明生命科学园

4. Guangming Life Science Park

Guangming Science City, Guangming District · Research teams + life-science ecosystem + government-supported infrastructure

⚡ Fun fact

Guangming Science City covers roughly 99 km² in northern Shenzhen and is positioned as a municipal-level core for information science, materials science and life sciences — hosting major scientific infrastructure, provincial laboratories and two research-oriented universities. The visit will meet on-site research teams and look at the government-supported infrastructure that anchors the life-science ecosystem (housing, talent schemes, shared facilities). The briefing deliberately describes this as a park visit, not a single-lab demo.[GM]
Read more

🔍 Why it matters

  • Guangming Science City (~99 km²) is positioned as a municipal-level core for information science, materials science, life sciences — hosting major scientific infrastructure, provincial labs, and two research-oriented universities.
  • The briefing scopes this stop at park level — meet on-site research teams and look at the government-supported infrastructure that anchors the ecosystem (space, talent schemes, shared facilities).
  • For NCIS, the relevance is translational: how a science-city model helps biotech / medtech companies move from research to clinical pilots.

🔍 What to observe

Park scope
~99 km², info / materials / life sciences focus — what tenants fit where
Anchor tenants
Which universities, provincial labs, and resident companies are on-site
Government support
Land, subsidies, talent housing, shared facilities — what is genuinely public vs. per-tenant
Translational path
How research moves from lab to clinical pilot under park-level rules
Talent strategy
How they attract and retain life-science talent — housing, visas, schools

💬 Questions to ask

1For a company entering the park, what is the typical journey from lab idea to first clinical pilot — and how much of that is government-enabled?
2Which shared facilities (wet lab, imaging, AI compute, GMP) do tenants rely on most?
3How do you coordinate between the two research universities, the provincial labs, and the resident companies?
4What regulatory fast paths (ethics, IRB, hospital handoff) exist for clinical translation?
5What is the biggest bottleneck today — funding, talent, clinical access, or regulation?
6How do you handle IP and data-sharing between university research and resident companies?
7For a Singapore team exploring cross-border collaboration, what is the realistic on-ramp?
8What 3 things would you tell a city trying to build a similar science-city model?

🇸🇬 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: This stop is about how a city builds the plumbing for life-science translation — not about a single AI product. That is a transferable discipline.

  • Shared wet-lab + imaging facilities as a public good
  • Talent-housing + visa schemes to attract life-science founders
  • Provincial / municipal lab governance model
  • Cross-border clinical translation pipelines (Shenzhen ↔ Singapore)
  • Science-city financing instruments (subsidies, co-investment funds)
For NCIS / Singapore, the honest question at Guangming is not "can we copy this?" but "which 2-3 pieces of this science-city plumbing (shared facilities, talent schemes, translation fast-paths) could realistically be replicated — or opened as a cross-border channel between Singapore and Shenzhen?"
XtPi
晶泰医疗

6. XtalPi (晶泰医疗)

Times Technology Building, Shennan Ave, Shenzhen · AI + robotics platform for scientific research and cancer screening applications

⚡ Fun fact

Founded in 2014 by 3 MIT quantum physicists,[16] XtalPi raised $318.8M in Series C in 2020 (co-led by SoftBank Vision Fund 2 and PICC Capital)[16] — one of the largest AI drug discovery rounds ever. Its ID4 platform combines quantum physics + AI + robotic wet-lab automation,[16][17] aiming to compress drug discovery timelines. It serves 16 of the world's top 20 pharmaceutical companies.[17] XtalPi helped optimise Pfizer's Paxlovid during COVID-19, compressing months of solid-form research into weeks.[18] Now listed on HKEX (stock: 2228.HK).[17]
Read more

🔍 Why it matters

  • XtalPi is the only visit on the trip focused on AI for pharmaceutical R&D, not directly on hospital operations - but it is the most relevant visit for NCIS oncology pipeline thinking if Singapore wants to participate in AI-driven drug discovery or translational research partnerships.
  • Founded in 2014 by three MIT quantum physicists; headquartered in Shenzhen (Times Technology Building, Shennan Avenue) with a US office in Cambridge, MA. Listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.[17]
  • Core thesis: combine quantum physics simulation + AI generative models + robotic wet-lab automation into a closed-loop discovery platform. XtalPi says the platform aims to compress drug discovery timelines.

🔍 What to observe

Closed-loop workflow
How AI dry-lab predictions feed robotic wet-lab experiments and back
Quantum + AI
Where quantum physics actually helps vs. classical ML would be enough
Oncology pipeline
Current oncology targets and stage of preclinical / clinical work
Big-pharma model
How XtalPi partners with global pharma - what they keep, what they share
Automation
What the autonomous lab actually does end-to-end
Data & IP
Who owns generated molecules, data, and IP

💬 Questions to ask

1For an oncology target, how do you decide AI generation vs. traditional medicinal chemistry?
2How do you validate AI-generated molecules before they enter animal studies?
3Where does quantum physics actually outperform classical AI in your pipeline?
4What is the realistic timeline from AI-generated molecule to IND filing today?
5For a hospital partner (NCIS), what is the lowest-friction collaboration model?
6How do you handle clinical data linkage if XtalPi partners with hospital systems?
7What IP and data-sharing terms are realistic for a Singapore partner?
8What does XtalPi NOT do well today, where classical pharma CROs still win?

🇸🇬 Singapore / NCIS translation

Why this matters: NCIS will not run an AI drug discovery platform, but Singapore could plausibly partner with one - and XtalPi is the most credible Shenzhen candidate. The questions we ask here determine whether a future MoU is realistic.

  • AI-driven preclinical candidate generation for NCIS-relevant oncology targets
  • Singapore clinical-trial partnership model (NCIS provides patient access, XtalPi provides molecules)
  • Cross-border IP & data governance for hospital-pharma R&D
  • AI validation framework before any human exposure
  • Realistic timeline from AI-generated molecule to first-in-human study
For NCIS, the value of the XtalPi visit is not to copy the platform but to map the partnership model - what would a realistic Singapore-Shenzhen oncology AI R&D collaboration actually look like, what data/IP terms are required, and which 1-2 NCIS-relevant targets could be a credible first pilot. If XtalPi is interested in Singapore clinical access, GMIO/NCIS should be ready with a concrete proposal within 30 days of return.

📌 Must-Ask Questions Across All Visits

Keep these in your notebook and reuse at every site.

A. Scaling beyond pilot

  1. What was the first AI use case that successfully scaled?
  2. What made it scale: technology, leadership, workflow, funding, or governance?
  3. How many AI pilots did not scale, and what were the common reasons?

B. Integration

  1. Is AI embedded inside the core clinical system, or a separate platform?
  2. How do you integrate AI with EMR / HIS / PACS / LIS / CRM / appointment systems?
  3. How do you avoid creating another silo?

C. Governance

  1. Who approves clinical AI use?
  2. How do you validate accuracy before go-live?
  3. How do you monitor performance after go-live?
  4. What happens when AI gives a wrong recommendation?

D. Adoption

  1. How do you convince clinicians to trust AI?
  2. Is AI optional, recommended, or mandatory in workflow?
  3. How do you manage alert fatigue or over-reliance?

E. Data

  1. What data is needed for AI to work well?
  2. How do you handle incomplete, inconsistent, or unstructured data?
  3. How do you handle patient consent and data sharing?

F. Singapore applicability

  1. If a healthcare system is bound by a major EMR vendor contract and central IT governance, what AI implementation model would you recommend?
  2. What can be done safely as a first step without disrupting enterprise systems?
  3. What should not be attempted too early?
  4. What capability must be built internally, instead of depending fully on vendors?

📝 What to Pay Attention To During the Trip

Don't just record "what AI they have" - record these five things instead:

What to captureWhy it matters
Use caseWhat problem does the AI solve?
WorkflowWhere exactly does it sit in the user journey?
IntegrationWhat system / data does it connect to?
GovernanceWho approves, monitors, and owns it?
OutcomeWhat measurable benefit was achieved?

📊 Suggested Post-Trip Report Structure

  1. Executive Summary - key learnings, what's relevant to NCIS/NUHS, what's not directly transferable
  2. Observations by Organisation - Tencent, Ping An, Shenzhen People's Hospital, Guangming Life Science Park, XtalPi
  3. Key Themes - AI at scale needs workflow integration; data/platform maturity beats model hype; governance must be designed early; AI must solve operational pain, not just impress leadership; Singapore must adapt, not copy
  4. Singapore / NCIS Applicability - what can be done in Epic/MyChart, what needs Synapxe governance, what can be sandboxed, what needs enterprise architecture approval
  5. Recommended Follow-Up Actions - strengthen GMIO GROSS / Epic PCR AI Agent, identify 2-3 NCIS oncology AI use cases, explore AI patient navigation + smart ops dashboard, start governance discussion with Synapxe early

🌟 Top 5 Takeaways to Aim For

  1. How Shenzhen chooses AI use cases that are worth scaling
  2. How AI is embedded into workflow instead of sitting outside the system
  3. How they govern AI safety, cybersecurity, privacy, and clinical accountability
  4. How they measure outcome and ROI after go-live
  5. How Singapore can adapt the model within Epic contract and Synapxe governance constraints
